name: Network Zone
description: Network Zone represents a defined network boundary used to group IP addresses or ranges to control access, traffic flows, and apply policy-based segmentation. It functions as a logical security boundary to separate trust levels (for example, DMZ, internal, guest, or management networks), enabling enforcement of access control lists, firewall rules, routing policies, and network access control (NAC). As a security boundary it does not itself store business data but carries, forwards, and can be used to restrict or permit data flows; therefore misconfiguration or compromise of the zone can expose all traffic and connected assets. Integration points typically include firewalls, routers, switches, NAC systems, VPN concentrators, IDS/IPS, logging/monitoring collectors, and connected hosts or services. Dependencies include correct device configuration, secure management channels, up-to-date device firmware, and monitoring/visibility tools. No inheritance or class metadata was provided for this component.

name: Kubernetes namespace
description: This component represents a Kubernetes namespace, a logical partition that establishes a security boundary within a cluster. It isolates namespaced resources (Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps, Pods), scopes names to avoid collisions, and enables enforcement of resource quotas, Role-Based Access Control (RBAC), and NetworkPolicies. The namespace integrates with the Kubernetes API server, admission controllers, and network plugins, and depends on the control plane and cluster nodes. While it provides strong isolation for namespaced workloads, it does not isolate cluster-wide resources (Nodes, PersistentVolumes, CRDs), making correct configuration critical to prevent privilege escalation, lateral movement, and denial-of-service scenarios.

name: Kubernetes Pod
description: A Kubernetes Pod encapsulates one or more containers as the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes, managing container lifecycles, shared networking, and storage within a cluster. Pods enforce isolation boundaries through namespaces, cgroups, and security contexts, and rely on the Kubernetes API server, kubelet, and container runtime interface (CRI) for orchestration. They integrate with higher-level controllers (Deployments, StatefulSets) and security mechanisms (RBAC, admission controllers, network policies) to manage authentication, authorization, and network segmentation. Misconfigurations or vulnerable workloads in Pods can compromise confidentiality, integrity, and availability of applications and the underlying host.

name: Amazon Web services
description: Amazon Web Services (AWS) is a comprehensive cloud service provider offering virtualized compute, storage, database, networking, and machine learning capabilities. It serves as a security boundary isolating customer workloads from public networks while providing identity and access management, encryption, logging, and monitoring features. AWS processes, stores, and transmits sensitive customer data across regions and integrates with customer VPCs, on-premises networks, and third-party services via APIs and management consoles. Under the shared responsibility model, AWS secures the underlying infrastructure while customers configure security controls. The platform’s extensive service portfolio and integration points introduce risks from misconfigurations, compromised credentials, unpatched services, and supply-chain threats.

name: Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service
description: Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) is a fully managed container orchestration service that provides a highly available and scalable Kubernetes control plane. EKS manages the Kubernetes API server, etcd cluster, and control plane components, enabling users to focus on deploying and scaling containerized applications. It integrates with AWS IAM for authentication and authorization, VPC networking for cluster isolation, and CloudWatch for logging and monitoring. Serving as a security boundary, EKS separates the managed control plane from customer-managed worker nodes within user-defined network architectures. The service handles cluster configuration data, application workloads, Kubernetes Secrets (backed by AWS KMS), and interacts with other AWS services such as ECR, ELB, and CloudTrail.

name: Amazon Virtual Private Cloud
description: The Amazon Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) provides an isolated virtual network environment within AWS, enabling users to define IP address ranges, subnets, route tables, security groups, network ACLs, and gateways. It functions as a security boundary that segments workloads, controls data in transit, and integrates with AWS services for private connectivity and monitoring. Proper configuration is critical to enforce network-level access controls, prevent unauthorized ingress and egress, and maintain visibility through flow logs and endpoint policies.

name: GitHub
description: GitHub is a cloud-hosted code repository and collaboration platform providing version control, issue tracking, CI/CD integration, and API access. It handles source code, documentation, dependencies, and workflow definitions across public and private repositories. As an external security boundary, it enforces access controls, credential management, and integrates with internal pipelines, requiring robust configuration, secret protection, and supply chain safeguards.

name: Amazon Lambda Service
description: Amazon Lambda Service is a serverless Function as a Service (FaaS) platform that executes user-provided code in ephemeral, isolated containers in response to events. It abstracts server provisioning and scaling, automatically handling resource allocation based on workload. Lambda functions integrate with AWS services (API Gateway, S3, DynamoDB, EventBridge) as event sources and can process or transform data before routing results to downstream services. As a security boundary, Lambda isolates each execution from other tenants and AWS resources, but relies on fine-grained IAM permissions, secure configuration of event sources, correct environment variable handling, and managed dependencies to maintain confidentiality, integrity, and availability of customer workloads.

name: AWS Subnet
description: An AWS Subnet is a logical network segment within an Amazon VPC that provides IP address isolation, traffic control via route tables and network ACLs, and acts as a security boundary between public and private resources. It organizes workloads by trust level, integrates with Internet Gateways, NAT Gateways, VPC Peering, Transit Gateways, and attaches Security Groups per resource. Subnets handle both inbound and outbound traffic, enforce network-level controls, and depend on correctly configured ACLs, route tables, and monitoring services to maintain confidentiality, integrity, and availability of hosted workloads.
